Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: We are given an event log table event_log with columns case_id activity and timestamp We need to filter out cases ie case_id that have more than 6 events and return all events from the remaining cases

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: First we can use a common table expression or subquery to count the number of events per case_id Then we can filter the case_ids that have a count <= 6 Finally we join this filtered list of case_id back to the original table to get the events for these cases

Name: Hypothesis Generation - IND
Text: Alternatively we can use a window function to compute the count per case_id and then filter

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: We can use a window function to compute the count for each row based on its case_id and then filter the rows where the count is <= 6

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - IND
Text: Lets write both and then choose the one that is more efficient or clear

Name: Validation - PE
Text: However note the window function in Method 2 will compute the count for every row and then we filter This might be less efficient if the table is large and most cases have more than 6 events because we compute the count for every row and then discard many

Name: Abductive Reasoning - IND
Text: But in DuckDB the window function is computed in one pass and then filtered so it might be acceptable

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: The requirement is to return the events from cases with <=6 events We do not need the event_count in the output so we can adjust

Name: Backtracking - PE
Text: The problem says returns only the events so we should return the original columns

Name: Backtracking - PE
Text: The table might have more columns We are told it has at least the following columns so we should return all columns of the event_log

Name: Hypothesis Generation - IND
Text: DuckDB supports the EXCLUDE clause in SELECT so that is an option

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - PE
Text: Let me stick with Method 1 because it is more standard in SQL and does not require an extra column in the result

Name: Validation - PE
Text: If there are cases with exactly 6 events we want them so we use <=6

Name: Hypothesis Generation - IND
Text: Alternatively we can use a subquery in the WHERE clause SELECT FROM event_log e WHERE e case_id IN SELECT case_id FROM event_log GROUP BY case_id HAVING COUNT <= 6

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - PE
Text: I think the IN method is more readable

Name: Validation - IND
Text: The subquery is independent and will be computed once then used to filter

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - PE
Text: Since the problem does not specify the size we can write the most straightforward

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Final query SELECT FROM event_log WHERE case_id IN SELECT case_id FROM event_log GROUP BY case_id HAVING COUNT <= 6 This returns all columns for the events in cases with at most 6 events

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Let me test with a simple example Case 1 has 2 events <=6 case 2 has 7 events >6 We want only case 1 Our query should return the two events of case 1 The subquery will return case_id 1 and then the outer query returns the events with case_id 1 So it works
